title: The Second Flotilla, the First Deal, and the Inflation That Won't Quit
subhead: Gaza flotilla sets sail again from Marmaris, Belgium locks in nine defense agreements with Ankara, the central bank concedes that war has shattered its inflation targets, and Turkey-Armenia take a tangible step toward direct trade — while the PKK peace process stalls and Ankara drafts a maritime claims bill that could reignite Aegean tensions.

sections.heading: Gaza Flotilla II Departs Turkey
sections.body:
Fifty-four vessels carrying over 500 activists sailed from Marmaris on Thursday as the Global Sumud Flotilla launched its second attempt to breach the Israeli blockade of Gaza. The first attempt in April saw 20 vessels intercepted by the Israeli Navy near Crete on April 29; 173 activists were deposited on Greek shores, and two steering committee members — Saif Abu Keshek and Thiago Avila — were detained in Israel. Abu Keshek, deported on Sunday, announced the relaunch at a press conference Wednesday: "We sail one day before the Nakba, not only to commemorate the anniversary of the Nakba, but to act on it."

Israel's Foreign Ministry called the flotilla a propaganda stunt carrying no humanitarian aid. Not all vessels from the first attempt are rejoining; Greenpeace's Arctic Sunrise remained near Crete. The flotilla's first iteration had grown from 39 ships departing Spain on April 15 to 56 vessels by the time it reached Italy, absorbing a 25-ship contingent before its interception.

The departure puts Turkey in an awkward position: Ankara has not formally backed the flotilla but has also not prevented it from staging out of Turkish ports. The timing — Nakba Day eve — ensures maximum media coverage and maximum diplomatic friction with Israel at a moment when Erdogan is simultaneously threatening military action against Israel and positioning Turkey as a regional mediator on the Iran ceasefire.
sections.whyItMatters: The flotilla forces a choice Ankara has been avoiding: either intercept or restrict the vessels and lose credibility with its domestic and pro-Palestinian constituencies, or allow the departure and absorb another diplomatic confrontation with Israel and its Western backers. Every prior flotilla incident has escalated — the 2010 Mavi Marmara raid killed ten activists and froze Turkey-Israel relations for six years.

sections.heading: Belgium Signs Nine Defense Deals, Backs Turkey for EU SAFE Program
sections.body:
Belgian Defense Minister Theo Francken announced nine defense-industrial agreements signed with Turkey during a four-day economic mission led by Queen Mathilde, calling the outcome "a really big step forward" toward a long-term strategic defense partnership. Six agreements were signed in Ankara on Wednesday and three in Istanbul on Monday. Around 40 additional agreements were prepared for signing, covering defense, biotechnology, health sciences, agriculture, and food processing.

Francken called Turkey's defense industry "a role model for me" and said Belgium was interested in purchasing Turkish drones, subject to European procurement rules. Belgium also expressed support for Turkey's access to the EU's €150 billion SAFE initiative for defense capability strengthening. "The strategic importance of Turkey in the rearmament and defense-industrial strengthening of Europe is obvious," Francken said. "Europe cannot afford to neglect the huge potential here." He also agreed to let Turkish officers study at Belgium's Royal Military Academy in Brussels.

The delegation of 450+ participants — including dozens of defense companies, research institutions, and universities — is the largest Belgian economic mission to Turkey in years. Bilateral trade stands at roughly $8 billion annually. Francken explicitly pushed back against customs tariffs on Turkey and rejected calls for an independent European nuclear deterrent, arguing that Europe should strengthen conventional capabilities within NATO's existing framework.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most concrete institutional defense partnership Turkey has secured with an EU member state in years. Belgium's explicit backing for Turkish access to the SAFE program — a €150 billion EU defense fund — is significant because Turkey's participation has been blocked by Greece and Cyprus through PESCO's unanimity rule. A single ally advocating inclusion doesn't break the veto, but it creates a precedent and a coalition-building starting point. The drone purchase interest also signals that European states are increasingly treating Turkey's defense industry as a procurement source, not just a geopolitical problem.

sections.heading: Central Bank Concedes: War Has Shattered the Inflation Framework
sections.body:
CBRT Governor Fatih Karahan presented the bank's second quarterly inflation report of 2026 on Thursday, and the numbers tell the story of a framework under severe strain. Year-end inflation targets were raised from 16% to 24% for 2026, from 9% to 15% for 2027, and from 8% to 9% for 2028. The forecast moved from a range (15-21% in February) to a point estimate of 26% for end-2026 — above the new target and slightly below market expectations of 27.53%.

April monthly inflation came in at 4.18%, pushing annual inflation to 32.37% — up from 30.65% in January. Annual energy inflation surged 19 percentage points to 47%, driven by oil and natural gas prices. The bank's oil price assumption was revised to $89.4/barrel for 2026 and $75.4 for 2027, up from earlier estimates. Food inflation expectations were revised upward to 26.3% for the year.

The bank abandoned its forecast band communication, with Karahan arguing that in periods of supply shocks and structural breaks, traditional forecast ranges are insufficient. The key policy shift: "All options remain on the table," Karahan said — including a rate hike, though he signaled reluctance to widen the interest rate corridor's upper bound, which would push up corporate lending rates. The policy rate remains at 37%, held steady for two consecutive meetings after a 100-basis-point cut in January.
sections.whyItMatters: The central bank's revised framework is essentially an acknowledgment that the Iran war has broken the disinflation program that Finance Minister Şimşek built on a $65/barrel oil assumption. Oil at ~$100 means the program's foundations no longer hold. The shift from range to point estimate and from forecast bands to risk-focused communication is not just a presentational change — it's a concession that historical volatility models can't capture the current environment. The real risk isn't the rate itself (37% is already tight) but whether reserve depletion and current account pressure force a credibility-damaging policy shift.

sections.heading: PKK Peace Process Stalls as Iran War Diverts Ankara's Attention
sections.body:
Just two weeks after Turkey's parliament issued recommendations to advance the Kurdish peace process, the Iran war has brought negotiations to what a Reuters analysis describes as "near standstill." PKK Executive Committee member Murat Karayilan accused the Turkish government of "effectively stopping the process," citing the continued isolation of jailed PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan, who has not met DEM Party representatives since March 27. DEM Party foreign relations spokesperson Ebru Gunay called the communication cutoff "a serious problem given the sensitivity of the process."

On the government side, Erdogan insists the process remains on track, saying Turkey has "passed the 18th month of the process and succeeded in overcoming many critical obstacles calmly." Coalition partner Devlet Bahceli — whose nationalist MHP party unexpectedly backs the initiative — proposed granting Ocalan a formal role as "Coordinator for the Peace Process and Politicization," a striking overture from a leader who once called Ocalan a "baby killer."

The PKK announced its dissolution and end of armed conflict in May 2025, carried out a symbolic weapons handover in Sulaymaniyah in July, but has since insisted that legislative reforms must accompany disarmament — including changes to prosecutions of Kurdish politicians and legal guarantees for PKK fighters. Ankara insists on full disarmament first. Kurdish frustration is mounting on both sides.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has given Ankara a reason to stall — and the PKK a reason to doubt the government's commitment. The Foreign Affairs analysis notes that the conflict threatens to disrupt the peace process by emboldening Kurdish factions and complicating Ankara's calculations. Bahceli's Ocalan proposal is the most creative attempt to break the deadlock, but it remains a nationalist leader's overture without implementing legislation behind it. Every month without progress increases the risk that the process collapses entirely — and a collapsed peace process during a regional war is a worst-case scenario for Turkish domestic stability.

sections.heading: Turkey-Armenia: The Border Opens for Trade
sections.body:
Turkey lifted customs restrictions on Armenia on Wednesday, allowing goods shipped through third countries to list Turkey or Armenia as their final destination or point of origin — ending a 33-year prohibition that dated to Turkey's 1993 border closure in solidarity with Azerbaijan. Armenia called it "an important step toward the establishment of full and normalized relations."

The customs change is one of several advancing normalization tracks. Turkey and Armenia also agreed to restore the medieval Ani Bridge across the Arpacay River border, signed by special representatives Serdar Kilic and Ruben Rubinyan on the sidelines of the European Political Community summit. Vice President Cevdet Yilmaz met Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan in Yerevan — the first high-level Turkish visit to Armenia in nearly two decades. Direct Istanbul-Yerevan flights launched in March; the Alican-Margara border crossing is 90% complete.

Ankara's push is tied to timing: Armenia holds parliamentary elections on June 7, and Pashinyan's Civil Contract party leads with 26.7% — a narrow plurality that could cost him his majority. Turkey wants Pashinyan in power to sustain both the Turkey-Armenia track and the parallel Armenia-Azerbaijan peace process, including the TRIPP corridor through Syunik linking Azerbaijan to Nakhchivan.

The Iran war and Hormuz disruption have given Ankara an additional motive: the Middle Corridor — a trans-Caspian trade route linking China to Europe via Central Asia, the South Caucasus, and Turkey — gains value with every day Hormuz stays disrupted. An open Armenia-Turkey border makes that corridor shorter and more viable.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most tangible step in Turkey-Armenia normalization since the process launched in 2022. The customs change isn't symbolic — it removes a real barrier to direct trade that has forced shipments through Georgia or Iran for three decades. Combined with the border crossing construction and the Ani Bridge restoration, the two countries are building the physical infrastructure of normalization before the diplomatic infrastructure exists. The risk: if Pashinyan loses his majority on June 7, every track freezes.

sections.heading: Maritime Claims Bill Drafted as Aegean Tensions Loom
sections.body:
Bloomberg reports that Turkey is preparing legislation to formalize its maritime jurisdiction claims in disputed areas of the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean, marking the first time Ankara would codify its claims into domestic law. The bill would assert Turkish rights over potential natural gas reserves in contested waters and strengthen jurisdictional claims that have until now existed only in diplomatic notes and naval deployments.

Erdogan ally Devlet Bahceli warned in parliament that any move ignoring Turkish maritime claims or Turkish Cypriot rights would prompt a "strong response," while asserting that "Turkey is not a country that seeks tension." The bill has not yet been formally submitted to parliament, and its introduction timeline is unclear.

Washington has encouraged dialogue between Athens and Ankara. The EU has previously warned Turkey over hydrocarbon exploration in disputed waters. For Greece and Cyprus, the legislation would be read as a direct challenge to their maritime exclusive economic zones and continental shelf claims — the same disputes that drove Turkish naval confrontations with Greek and French warships in 2020.
sections.whyItMatters: Codifying maritime claims into domestic law is a significant escalation from asserting them in diplomatic notes or naval deployments — it makes retreat politically costly and creates legal frameworks that could justify enforcement actions. The timing is notable: Turkey is simultaneously seeking deeper EU defense integration (Belgium's SAFE advocacy) and drafting legislation that Greece and Cyprus will read as a provocation. The two tracks work against each other.

sections.heading: Hormuz Squeeze: Oil Flows Down 30%, EIA Launches Monitoring
sections.body:
The EIA reported that crude oil and fuel flows through the Strait of Hormuz fell by nearly 6 million barrels per day in Q1 2026 — a nearly 30% decline from pre-war levels. The agency launched a new quarterly report tracking flows through major chokepoints, reflecting the structural gravity of the disruption. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned that Hormuz disruption could push oil market recovery into 2027.

Oil prices edged up Thursday as markets focused on the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing for any sign of progress on the Iran conflict. Meanwhile, the CBRT's revised oil price assumptions ($89.4/barrel for 2026) reflect a reality where Turkey — which imports over 90% of its crude and 96% of its natural gas — faces an energy bill that could push the current account deficit well beyond the historical average.

IEA chief Fatih Birol — himself Turkish — has publicly called for a new Basra-Ceyhan pipeline to reduce global Hormuz dependence, a proposal that would make Turkey's Mediterranean terminal the primary bypass route for Iraqi crude. The existing Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline runs at roughly 250,000 bpd with potential to reach 650,000 bpd. Iraq has started construction on the $1.5 billion Basra-Haditha pipeline targeting 2.5 million bpd that could route to Ceyhan, Baniyas, or Aqaba.
sections.whyItMatters: The 30% decline in Hormuz flows is the structural backdrop against which all of Turkey's economic and strategic calculations now run. Every month at $90+ oil costs Turkey billions in reserves and undermines the disinflation program. The Ceyhan opportunity is real but moving slowly — and the maritime claims bill targeting Aegean waters could complicate exactly the international partnerships Turkey needs to expand Ceyhan into a Hormuz bypass terminal.

bottomLine: May 14 brought three distinct kinds of developments for Turkey: diplomatic gains, economic headwinds, and familiar tensions. The Belgium defense agreements — nine signed, dozens more in the pipeline, plus explicit SAFE program advocacy — represent the most concrete EU-member-state defense partnership Turkey has built in years. The Turkey-Armenia customs normalization and Ani Bridge restoration are real physical steps in a process that has been mostly symbolic since 2022. Both tracks advance Turkey's strategic interests: defense integration with Europe and the Middle Corridor trade route through the South Caucasus. But the central bank's inflation report tells the other side of the story: targets revised up by 50% or more, forecast bands abandoned, and a governor explicitly acknowledging that the war has broken the disinflation framework. The PK K peace process has stalled, the Gaza flotilla has sailed again from Turkish waters, and a draft maritime claims bill threatens to antagonize the same Greece and Cyprus whose veto blocks Turkey's EU defense access. Turkey is building partnerships with one hand and writing legislation that undermines them with the other.
sources: Jerusalem Post (Gaza flotilla), AP News (Turkey-Armenia trade), Al Monitor (Turkey-Armenia bridge, Bahceli Ocalan proposal), Hindustan Times/AFP (Belgium-Turkey defense deals), Daily Sabah (CBRT inflation report, Belgian defense minister), ING (CBRT policy analysis), Foreign Affairs (Iran war threat to Turkey, PKK analysis), New Arab (PKK peace process), Greek Reporter (maritime claims bill), Bloomberg/EIA (Hormuz flows, Turkey maritime bill), Reuters (Turkey-Armenia trade, Hormuz alternatives), Defense News (Turkey EU defense role, PESCO)
